content:
# Acts Chapter 21 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Acts 21
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 251-253

---

## Summary Statistics
- **Total verses:** 40
- **Verses with disagreements:** 39
- **Total individual disagreements logged:** 108
- **Categories:** Word choice, structural/syntactic, interpretive expansion, theological/textual

---

## Full Disagreement Table

| Verse | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | "And as it-came-to-pass [for] us to-set-sail" | "And when it came about that we set sail" | "After we had torn ourselves away from them, we put out to sea" | "And when it came about that we set sail" | LOGOS natural English for EGENETO + infinitive; PNEUMA "torn ourselves away" is vivid but interpretive for APOSPASTHENTES |
| 1 | "having-been-withdrawn from them" | "having parted from them" | "torn ourselves away from them" | "having parted from them" | LOGOS neutral rendering; EMET "withdrawn" is passive-awkward; PNEUMA "torn ourselves away" captures APOSPAO force but is too dramatic for consensus |
| 1 | "having-run-a-straight-course we-came to Cos" | "we came by a straight course to Cos" | "sailed a straight course to Cos" | "we came by a straight course to Cos" | LOGOS smooth; EMET hyphenated compound awkward; PNEUMA drops "came" verb |
| 1 | "and the next-[day] to Rhodes, and from-there to Patara" | "and the next day to Rhodes, and from there to Patara" | "The next day we reached Rhodes, and from there went on to Patara" | "and the next day to Rhodes, and from there to Patara" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA restructures into new sentence with added verb "reached" |
| 2 | "having-gone-aboard we-set-sail" | "we went aboard and set sail" | "we boarded it and set sail" | "we went aboard and set sail" | LOGOS natural; PNEUMA "boarded it" adds pronoun; EMET hyphenated participle |
| 3 | "having-sighted Cyprus and having-left it on-the-left" | "having sighted Cyprus and leaving it on the left" | "We came in sight of Cyprus, passed it on our left" | "having sighted Cyprus and leaving it on the left" | LOGOS preserves participial chain; PNEUMA restructures into main clauses; EMET double-participle awkward |
| 3 | "we-were-sailing to Syria and came-down to Tyre" | "we sailed to Syria and landed at Tyre" | "sailed on to Syria. We landed at Tyre" | "we sailed to Syria and landed at Tyre" | LOGOS "landed" best for KATHLTHOMEN in nautical context; EMET "came-down" too literal; PNEUMA splits into two sentences |
| 3 | "for there the ship was unloading the cargo" | "for there the ship was to unload its cargo" | "where the ship was to unload its cargo" | "for there the ship was to unload its cargo" | LOGOS captures imperfective intent; EMET implies already unloading; PNEUMA "where" replaces "for there" |
| 4 | "having-found the disciples" | "having sought out the disciples" | "We sought out the disciples there" | "having sought out the disciples" | LOGOS "sought out" better renders ANEURONTES (finding after searching); EMET "found" loses the search element; PNEUMA restructures |
| 4 | "who to-Paul were-saying through the Spirit not to-set-foot in Jerusalem" | "they kept telling Paul through the Spirit not to set foot in Jerusalem" | "Through the Spirit they kept urging Paul not to set foot in Jerusalem" | "they kept telling Paul through the Spirit not to set foot in Jerusalem" | LOGOS word order and imperfective "kept telling" best preserve ELEGON; PNEUMA "urging" is stronger than "telling"; EMET relative clause "who" is awkward in English |
| 4 | "not to-set-foot in Jerusalem" | "not to set foot in Jerusalem" | "not to set foot in Jerusalem" | "not to set foot in Jerusalem" | All three agree on EPIBAINO rendering |
| 5 | "And when it-came-to-pass [for] us to-complete the days" | "And when it came about that we had completed the days" | "But when our time was up" | "And when it came about that we had completed the days" | LOGOS renders EGENETO construction naturally; PNEUMA "our time was up" is colloquial paraphrase; EMET bracketed supplements awkward |
| 5 | "having-gone-out we-were-journeying, all accompanying us with wives and children" | "we departed and went on our way, with all of them, together with wives and children, accompanying us" | "we left and continued on our way. All of them, with their wives and children, walked us out of the city" | "we departed and went on our way, with all of them, together with wives and children, accompanying us" | LOGOS preserves single sentence with participial accompaniment; PNEUMA splits and adds "walked us out" |
| 5 | "unto outside the city" | "until we were outside the city" | "out of the city" | "until we were outside the city" | LOGOS clarifies EOS EXO; PNEUMA simplifies; EMET "unto outside" is unnatural |
| 5 | "having-knelt-down upon the shore, having-prayed" | "having knelt down on the beach, we prayed" | "There on the beach we knelt down and prayed together" | "having knelt down on the shore, we prayed" | SOPHIA blends: "shore" (EMET/LOGOS AIGIALON) + LOGOS structure; PNEUMA adds "together" not in text; "beach" (LOGOS) and "shore" both valid -- "shore" chosen for consistency |
| 6 | "we-greeted one-another" | "having said farewell to one another" | "we said our goodbyes to each other" | "having said farewell to one another" | LOGOS "said farewell" best for ASPASAMENOI in departure context; EMET "greeted" loses the farewell nuance; PNEUMA "goodbyes" is informal |
| 6 | "we-went-up into the ship" | "we went aboard the ship" | "climbed aboard the ship" | "we went aboard the ship" | LOGOS natural; PNEUMA "climbed" is interpretive; EMET "went-up into" is literal but awkward |
| 6 | "those returned to the own-[things]" | "they returned to their own homes" | "they turned back home" | "they returned to their own homes" | LOGOS supplies "homes" for TA IDIA naturally; EMET "own-things" is hyper-literal; PNEUMA "turned back" acceptable |
| 7 | "the voyage having-completed from Tyre" | "having completed the voyage from Tyre" | "Continuing our voyage from Tyre" | "having completed the voyage from Tyre" | LOGOS participial placement natural; PNEUMA "continuing" changes sense (DIANUSANTES means completing); EMET absolute genitive awkward |
| 7 | "arrived at Ptolemais" | "arrived at Ptolemais" | "put in at Ptolemais" | "arrived at Ptolemais" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "put in" is nautical idiom not in Greek |
| 7 | "having-greeted the brothers" | "having greeted the brothers" | "greeted the brothers and sisters" | "having greeted the brothers" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "and sisters" (not in Sinaiticus ADELPHOUS) |
| 7 | "we-remained one day with them" | "we remained with them one day" | "spent one day with them" | "we remained with them one day" | LOGOS word order natural; PNEUMA "spent" acceptable but loses EMEINAMEN; EMET word order slightly off |
| 8 | "on-the next-[day] having-gone-out" | "on the next day we departed" | "The next day we left" | "on the next day we departed" | LOGOS preserves temporal marker; PNEUMA "left" is casual; EMET bracketed supplement |
| 8 | "having-entered into the house of-Philip the evangelist, being of the seven" | "having entered the house of Philip the evangelist, who was one of the seven" | "We went to the home of Philip the evangelist, one of the Seven" | "having entered the house of Philip the evangelist, who was one of the seven" | LOGOS relative clause natural; PNEUMA "home" vs "house" and capitalizes "Seven"; EMET "being of" is stilted |
| 8 | "we-remained with him" | "we stayed with him" | "stayed with him" | "we stayed with him" | LOGOS/PNEUMA "stayed" more natural than EMET "remained" for EMEINEN in hospitality context |
| 9 | "to-this-[man] there-were four daughters, virgins, prophesying" | "this man had four virgin daughters who prophesied" | "He had four unmarried daughters who prophesied" | "this man had four virgin daughters who prophesied" | LOGOS renders PARTHENOI as "virgin" and restructures naturally; PNEUMA "unmarried" is interpretive weakening of PARTHENOI; EMET word order inverted |
| 10 | "[while] we-were-remaining for many days" | "as we were staying for many days" | "After we had been there a number of days" | "as we were staying for many days" | LOGOS preserves present participle EPIMENONTON; PNEUMA "a number of days" weakens PLEOUS ("many"); EMET brackets added |
| 10 | "a certain prophet came-down from Judea, by-name Agabus" | "a certain prophet named Agabus came down from Judea" | "a prophet named Agabus came down from Judea" | "a certain prophet named Agabus came down from Judea" | LOGOS word order; EMET "by-name" post-positioned awkwardly; PNEUMA drops "certain" (TIS) |
| 11 | "having-taken the belt of-Paul" | "having taken Paul's belt" | "took Paul's belt" | "having taken Paul's belt" | EMET/LOGOS agree on participial form; PNEUMA converts to finite verb |
| 11 | "having-bound his-own feet and hands" | "he bound his own feet and hands" | "tied his own hands and feet with it" | "he bound his own feet and hands" | LOGOS natural; PNEUMA reverses order to "hands and feet" and adds "with it"; EMET participial chain |
| 11 | "These-things says the Holy Spirit" | "Thus says the Holy Spirit" | "The Holy Spirit says this" | "Thus says the Holy Spirit" | LOGOS preserves prophetic formula TADE LEGEI; PNEUMA inverts subject-verb; EMET "These-things" is literal but less idiomatic |
| 11 | "The man whose this belt is -- thus shall-bind him in Jerusalem the Jews and shall-deliver-over [him] into [the] hands of-[the] Gentiles" | "The man whose belt this is -- so shall the Jews in Jerusalem bind him and deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles" | "The man who owns this belt -- this is how the Jewish leaders in Jerusalem will bind him and hand him over to the Gentiles" | "The man whose belt this is -- so shall the Jews in Jerusalem bind him and deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles" | LOGOS renders oracle naturally; PNEUMA "owns" for possessive and "Jewish leaders" (not in Greek -- just OI IOUDAIOI "the Jews") and "hand him over" is informal; EMET word order mirrors Greek but is unclear in English |
| 12 | "we-were-urging, both we and those of-the-place" | "both we and those of that place urged him" | "we and the local believers pleaded with Paul" | "both we and those of that place urged him" | LOGOS preserves PAREKALOUMEN; PNEUMA "local believers" interprets "those of the place" and "pleaded" is stronger than "urged"; EMET imperfective awkward |
| 13 | "What are-you-doing, weeping and breaking my heart?" | "What are you doing, weeping and breaking my heart?" | "What are you doing, weeping and breaking my heart like this?" | "What are you doing, weeping and breaking my heart?" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "like this" not in text |
| 13 | "For I, not only to-be-bound but also to-die in Jerusalem am holding-myself ready for the name of-the Lord Jesus" | "For I am ready not only to be bound but also to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus" | "I am ready not only to be bound but to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus" | "For I am ready not only to be bound but also to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus" | LOGOS word order and "For I am ready" best; PNEUMA drops "also" (KAI); EMET "holding-myself ready" is literal HETOIMOS ECHO but awkward |
| 14 | "[as] he-was not being-persuaded" | "since he would not be persuaded" | "When he would not be persuaded" | "since he would not be persuaded" | LOGOS "since" captures causal connection; PNEUMA "when" is temporal; EMET bracketed addition |
| 14 | "we-were-silent, having-said" | "we fell silent, saying" | "we gave up and said" | "we fell silent, saying" | LOGOS "fell silent" natural for HESUCHASAMEN; PNEUMA "gave up" is interpretive addition; EMET "were-silent, having-said" has participle in wrong aspect |
| 14 | "The will of-the Lord let-it-be-done" | "The will of the Lord be done" | "The Lord's will be done" | "The will of the Lord be done" | LOGOS formal jussive "be done" renders GINESTHO; PNEUMA possessive "Lord's" restructures genitive; EMET "let-it-be-done" is acceptable but less natural |
| 15 | "after the days these, having-packed-up" | "after these days, having made ourselves ready" | "After these days we packed up" | "after these days, having made ourselves ready" | LOGOS renders EPISKEUASAMENOI as "made ourselves ready"; EMET "packed-up" is one valid reading; PNEUMA converts to finite verb |
| 15 | "we-were-going-up to Jerusalem" | "we went up to Jerusalem" | "headed for Jerusalem" | "we went up to Jerusalem" | LOGOS aorist; PNEUMA "headed for" is informal; EMET imperfective tense |
| 16 | "there-came also [some] of-the disciples from Caesarea with us" | "some of the disciples from Caesarea also went with us" | "Some of the disciples from Caesarea came along with us" | "some of the disciples from Caesarea also went with us" | LOGOS natural English word order; PNEUMA "came along" is colloquial; EMET inverted construction |
| 16 | "bringing [us] to Mnason, a certain Cyprian, an-early disciple, with whom we-might-lodge" | "bringing us to Mnason of Cyprus, an early disciple, with whom we were to lodge" | "brought us to the home of Mnason of Cyprus, an early disciple, where we were to stay" | "bringing us to Mnason of Cyprus, an early disciple, with whom we were to lodge" | LOGOS preserves "with whom we were to lodge"; PNEUMA adds "home of" (not explicit) and "stay" for "lodge"; EMET "a certain Cyprian" literal but awkward |
| 17 | "the brothers received us gladly" | "the brothers received us gladly" | "the brothers and sisters welcomed us warmly" | "the brothers received us gladly" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "and sisters" and renders ASMENOS as "warmly" (it means "gladly") |
| 18 | "on-the following-[day] Paul was-going-in with us to James" | "on the following day Paul went in with us to James" | "The next day Paul went with us to see James" | "on the following day Paul went in with us to James" | LOGOS preserves "following day" (TE EPIOUSE); PNEUMA "next day" acceptable, adds "to see" (interpretive); EMET imperfective "was-going-in" |
| 18 | "all the elders were-present" | "all the elders were present" | "all the elders were there" | "all the elders were present" | EMET/LOGOS agree on "present" for PAREGENONTO; PNEUMA "there" is weaker |
| 19 | "he-was-reporting one-by-one each-[thing] which God did among the Gentiles through the ministry of-him" | "he began to relate one by one the things that God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry" | "gave a detailed account of everything God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry" | "he began to relate one by one the things that God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry" | LOGOS best renders EXEGEITO as "began to relate"; PNEUMA "detailed account" loses KATH HEN HEKASTON ("one by one each thing"); EMET "ministry of-him" inverted |
| 20 | "they-said to-him" | "they said to him" | "Then they said to him" | "they said to him" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "Then" (not in text) |
| 20 | "how-many tens-of-thousands there-are among the Jews of-the-[ones] having-believed" | "how many thousands there are among the Jews who have believed" | "how many thousands of Jews have come to believe" | "how many tens of thousands there are among the Jews who have believed" | SOPHIA retains "tens of thousands" for MURIADES -- the Greek specifically means multiples of 10,000, not generic "thousands." LOGOS/PNEUMA under-translate; EMET literal but awkward word order |
| 20 | "and all zealous-ones of-the law they-are" | "and they are all zealous for the law" | "and every one of them is zealous for the Law" | "and they are all zealous for the law" | LOGOS natural word order; PNEUMA "every one of them" expands PANTES; EMET inverted; PNEUMA capitalizes "Law" (not a Sinaiticus distinction) |
| 21 | "they-were-informed concerning you" | "they have been informed about you" | "they have been told about you" | "they have been informed about you" | LOGOS/PNEUMA prefer perfect tense; LOGOS "informed" more precise for KATECHETHESEN; PNEUMA "told" is casual |
| 21 | "apostasy from Moses you-teach all the Jews among the Gentiles" | "you teach all the Jews who are among the Gentiles apostasy from Moses" | "you teach all the Jews living among the Gentiles to abandon Moses" | "you teach all the Jews who are among the Gentiles apostasy from Moses" | LOGOS restructures for English clarity; PNEUMA "abandon" for APOSTASIA is interpretive; EMET Greek word order |
| 21 | "telling [them] not to-circumcise the children nor to-walk in the customs" | "telling them not to circumcise their children nor to walk according to the customs" | "telling them not to circumcise their children or follow the customs" | "telling them not to circumcise their children nor to walk according to the customs" | LOGOS "according to" for KATA natural; PNEUMA "follow" for PERIPATEIN (walk) is loose; EMET drops possessive |
| 22 | "What therefore is-it? Certainly a-multitude must come-together, for they-will-hear that you-have-come" | "What then is to be done? They will certainly hear that you have come" | "So what do we do? They will certainly hear that you have come" | "What then is to be done? They will certainly hear that you have come" | LOGOS renders TI OUN ESTIN with proper deliberative force; PNEUMA "So what do we do?" is colloquial; EMET includes "a-multitude must come-together" from Sinaiticus variant -- SOPHIA follows LOGOS shorter reading for the main text |
| 23 | "there-are to-us four men having a vow upon themselves" | "there are four men among us who have a vow upon themselves" | "We have four men who are under a vow" | "there are four men among us who have a vow upon themselves" | LOGOS "among us" renders HEMIN naturally; PNEUMA "under a vow" is acceptable but "have a vow upon themselves" is closer to ECHONTES EUCHEN EPH HEAUTON; EMET "to-us" is literal dative |
| 23 | "This therefore do which we-say to-you" | "Therefore do this that we tell you" | "Here is what we suggest" | "Therefore do this that we tell you" | LOGOS preserves imperative force; PNEUMA "suggest" weakens the directive OUN POIESON; EMET inverted word order |
| 24 | "these having-taken, be-purified with them" | "Take these men and purify yourself together with them" | "Take them along, go through the purification rite with them" | "Take these men and purify yourself together with them" | LOGOS explicit and clear; PNEUMA "purification rite" adds "rite" (not in text); EMET participial chain |
| 24 | "spend-on them so-that they-shall-shave the head" | "bear the expense for them so that they may shave their heads" | "pay for them to have their heads shaved" | "bear the expense for them so that they may shave their heads" | LOGOS "bear the expense" renders DAPANESON naturally; PNEUMA passive "have their heads shaved" restructures; EMET "spend-on" is literal |
| 24 | "all shall-know that [of] which they-have-been-informed concerning you [there] is nothing, but you-walk-orderly yourself also keeping the law" | "all will know that there is nothing in what they have been told about you, but that you yourself also walk in an orderly manner, keeping the law" | "everyone will know that the reports about you are groundless and that you yourself live in keeping with the Law" | "all will know that there is nothing in what they have been told about you, but that you yourself also walk in an orderly manner, keeping the law" | LOGOS most readable; PNEUMA "groundless" and "live" are interpretive; EMET syntax mirrors Greek but is unclear in English |
| 25 | "concerning the Gentiles having-believed, we sent, having-judged [that] they-[should] guard themselves from-both idol-sacrifice and blood and strangled and sexual-immorality" | "concerning the Gentiles who have believed, we have written, having decided that they should keep themselves from what is sacrificed to idols and from blood and from what is strangled and from sexual immorality" | "As for the Gentile believers, we have already written our decision: they should stay away from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from the meat of strangled animals, and from sexual immorality" | "concerning the Gentiles who have believed, we have written, having decided that they should keep themselves from what is sacrificed to idols and from blood and from what is strangled and from sexual immorality" | LOGOS precise rendering of fourfold decree; PNEUMA adds "food" and "meat of" (interpretive expansions), "already" not in text, "stay away" casual; EMET "we sent" for EPESTEILAMEN (we wrote/sent a letter) is less clear |
| 25 | "idol-sacrifice" | "what is sacrificed to idols" | "food sacrificed to idols" | "what is sacrificed to idols" | LOGOS renders EIDOLOTHUTOS most naturally; PNEUMA adds "food"; EMET compound noun |
| 25 | "strangled" | "what is strangled" | "the meat of strangled animals" | "what is strangled" | LOGOS "what is strangled" for PNIKTON; PNEUMA adds "meat of...animals"; EMET bare adjective |
| 26 | "on-the next day having-been-purified-with them" | "on the next day, having purified himself together with them" | "the next day Paul took the men, purified himself along with them" | "on the next day, having purified himself together with them" | LOGOS preserves participial structure; PNEUMA repeats "Paul took the men" from earlier clause; EMET passive voice |
| 26 | "was-entering into the temple, announcing the fulfillment of-the days of-the purification" | "he entered the temple, giving notice of the completion of the days of purification" | "went to the temple to give notice of the date when the days of purification would be completed" | "he entered the temple, giving notice of the completion of the days of purification" | LOGOS clean; PNEUMA adds "date" and restructures with "when...would be"; EMET "was-entering" imperfective |
| 26 | "until which was-offered for each one of-them the offering" | "until the offering was presented for each one of them" | "and the offering made for each of them" | "until the offering was presented for each one of them" | LOGOS "presented" for PROSHNECHTHE; PNEUMA drops "until" clause structure; EMET word order inverted |
| 27 | "the Jews from Asia, having-seen him in the temple, were-stirring-up all the crowd and laid the hands upon him" | "the Jews from Asia, having seen him in the temple, stirred up all the crowd and laid hands on him" | "some Jews from the province of Asia spotted Paul in the temple. They whipped the whole crowd into a frenzy and grabbed him" | "the Jews from Asia, having seen him in the temple, stirred up all the crowd and laid hands on him" | LOGOS preserves participial structure; PNEUMA "province of Asia" (adds "province"), "spotted" (casual), "whipped into a frenzy" (dramatic expansion), "grabbed" (informal for EPEBALON TAS CHEIRAS); EMET "were-stirring-up" imperfective |
| 28 | "Men, Israelites, help!" | "Men of Israel, help!" | "Men of Israel, help!" | "Men of Israel, help!" | LOGOS/PNEUMA render ANDRES ISRAELITAI naturally; EMET "Men, Israelites" preserves Greek apposition but is awkward |
| 28 | "This is the man, the-[one] against the people and the law and this place, all [men] everywhere teaching" | "This is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against the people and the law and this place" | "This is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against our people, our Law, and this sacred place" | "This is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against the people and the law and this place" | LOGOS restructures for clarity; PNEUMA adds possessive "our" twice and "sacred" (not in text -- HAGIOS appears only in next clause); EMET inverted word order |
| 28 | "and further also Greeks he-brought into the temple and has-defiled this holy place" | "and moreover he has also brought Greeks into the temple and has defiled this holy place" | "And on top of that, he has brought Greeks into the temple and defiled this holy ground!" | "and moreover he has also brought Greeks into the temple and has defiled this holy place" | LOGOS formal "moreover"; PNEUMA "on top of that" is colloquial, "holy ground" for HAGION TOPON this different from "holy place"; EMET word order |
| 29 | "they-had-seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with him, whom they-were-supposing that into the temple Paul brought-in" | "they had previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with him, whom they supposed that Paul had brought into the temple" | "They had previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with Paul and assumed Paul had brought him into the temple" | "they had previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with him, whom they supposed that Paul had brought into the temple" | LOGOS/PNEUMA add "previously" (PROEORAKO- prefix warrants it); PNEUMA "assumed" for ENOMIZON slightly weaker than "supposed"; EMET word order mirrors Greek |
| 29 | "they-were-supposing" | "they supposed" | "assumed" | "they supposed" | LOGOS aorist rendering; PNEUMA "assumed" is acceptable but slightly different nuance; EMET imperfective |
| 30 | "the whole city was-moved, and there-was a running-together of-the people" | "the whole city was stirred up, and the people rushed together" | "The whole city erupted. People came running from every direction" | "the whole city was stirred up, and the people rushed together" | LOGOS "stirred up" for EKINTHE and "rushed together" for SUNDROME natural; PNEUMA "erupted" is dramatic expansion, "from every direction" added; EMET "running-together" is literal compound |
| 30 | "having-seized Paul they-were-dragging him outside the temple" | "having seized Paul, they dragged him outside the temple" | "They seized Paul and dragged him out of the temple" | "having seized Paul, they dragged him outside the temple" | LOGOS preserves participial chain; PNEUMA converts to coordinate clauses; both valid |
| 30 | "the doors were-shut" | "the doors were shut" | "the gates were slammed shut behind him" | "the doors were shut" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "slammed" and "behind him" (not in text). THURAI = "doors" not "gates" |
| 31 | "a report went-up to-the commander of-the cohort that all Jerusalem is-in-confusion" | "a report came up to the tribune of the cohort that all Jerusalem was in confusion" | "word reached the commander of the Roman garrison that all Jerusalem was in chaos" | "a report came up to the commander of the cohort that all Jerusalem was in confusion" | LOGOS "tribune" is technical Latin term for CHILIARCHOS; SOPHIA uses "commander" as clearer English (with EMET/PNEUMA); PNEUMA adds "Roman" (not in text), "chaos" stronger than SUGCHUNNETAI |
| 31 | "they-were-seeking to-kill him" | "they were seeking to kill him" | "They were trying to kill him" | "while they were seeking to kill him" | EMET/LOGOS "seeking" for ZETOUNTON; PNEUMA "trying" acceptable; SOPHIA adds "while" for temporal clarity |
| 32 | "Who immediately having-taken soldiers and centurions ran-down upon them" | "he at once took soldiers and centurions and ran down to them" | "He immediately took soldiers and centurions and charged down to the crowd" | "he at once took soldiers and centurions and ran down to them" | LOGOS "at once" and "ran down to them" natural; PNEUMA "charged down to the crowd" adds intensity; EMET relative pronoun "Who" is awkward |
| 32 | "the-[ones] having-seen the commander and the soldiers ceased beating Paul" | "when they saw the tribune and the soldiers, they stopped beating Paul" | "When the mob saw the commander and his troops, they stopped beating Paul" | "when they saw the commander and the soldiers, they stopped beating Paul" | LOGOS temporal clause clear; PNEUMA "mob" and "his troops" are interpretive; EMET participial |
| 33 | "having-drawn-near the commander seized him and commanded [him] to-be-bound with two chains" | "the tribune drew near and took hold of him and ordered him to be bound with two chains" | "The commander came up, arrested Paul, and ordered him bound with two chains" | "the commander drew near and took hold of him and ordered him to be bound with two chains" | LOGOS "drew near and took hold" faithful; PNEUMA "arrested" interprets EPELABETO (took hold of); "commander" used per v.31 decision |
| 33 | "was-inquiring who he-might-be and what it-is he-has-done" | "he inquired who he might be and what he had done" | "Then he demanded to know who he was and what he had done" | "he inquired who he might be and what he had done" | LOGOS preserves optative "might be"; PNEUMA "demanded" stronger than EPUNTHANETO (inquired); EMET mixed tenses |
| 34 | "others were-shouting-out something other in the crowd" | "some in the crowd were shouting one thing and some another" | "in the crowd, some were shouting one thing, some another" | "some in the crowd were shouting one thing and some another" | LOGOS renders ALLO TI distributively; PNEUMA word order variation; EMET "something other" is literal but unclear |
| 34 | "not being-able to-know the certainty on-account-of the uproar" | "since he was unable to ascertain the facts because of the uproar" | "Since the commander could not get at the truth because of the uproar" | "since he was unable to ascertain the facts because of the uproar" | LOGOS "ascertain the facts" best for GNWNAI TO ASPHALES; PNEUMA "get at the truth" is colloquial; EMET "know the certainty" is literal but stilted |
| 34 | "he-commanded him to-be-led into the barracks" | "he ordered him to be brought into the barracks" | "he ordered Paul taken into the barracks" | "he ordered him to be brought into the barracks" | LOGOS full passive infinitive; PNEUMA drops "to be"; EMET "led" vs "brought" for AGESTHAI |
| 35 | "when he-came upon the steps, it-happened [that] he-was-being-carried by the soldiers" | "when he came upon the steps, it happened that he was being carried by the soldiers" | "When Paul reached the steps, the violence of the mob was so intense that the soldiers had to carry him" | "when he came upon the steps, it happened that he was being carried by the soldiers" | EMET/LOGOS agree on structure; PNEUMA restructures dramatically with "so intense that" |
| 35 | "on-account-of the violence of-the crowd" | "because of the violence of the crowd" | "the violence of the mob was so intense" | "because of the violence of the crowd" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "mob" interpretive, restructures causation |
| 36 | "the multitude of-the people was-following, crying-out" | "the multitude of the people kept following, crying out" | "The mass of people kept pressing in behind, screaming" | "the multitude of the people kept following, crying out" | LOGOS "kept following" captures imperfective; PNEUMA "mass" for PLETHOS acceptable, "pressing in behind" adds motion not in text, "screaming" stronger than KRAZONTES |
| 36 | "Away-with him!" | "Away with him!" | "Away with him!" | "Away with him!" | All three agree on AIRE AUTON rendering |
| 37 | "Paul says to-the commander" | "he said to the tribune" | "Paul said to the commander" | "he said to the commander" | SOPHIA follows "commander" throughout; EMET historic present "says"; LOGOS uses "tribune" |
| 37 | "Is-it-permitted for-me to-say something to you?" | "Is it permitted for me to say something to you?" | "May I say something to you?" | "Is it permitted for me to say something to you?" | EMET/LOGOS agree on formal register; PNEUMA "May I" is natural but loses EI EXESTIN formal construction |
| 37 | "Greek you-know?" | "Do you know Greek?" | "You speak Greek?" | "Do you know Greek?" | LOGOS interrogative natural; PNEUMA "speak" interprets GINOSKEIS ("know"); EMET mirrors Greek word order |
| 38 | "Are you not then the Egyptian, the-[one] before these days having-stirred-up and having-led-out into the wilderness the four-thousand men of-the Sicarii?" | "Are you not then the Egyptian who before these days stirred up a revolt and led the four thousand men of the Assassins out into the wilderness?" | "Then you are not the Egyptian who recently stirred up a revolt and led four thousand assassins out into the wilderness?" | "Are you not then the Egyptian who before these days stirred up a revolt and led the four thousand men of the Sicarii out into the wilderness?" | SOPHIA uses "Sicarii" (transliterated loanword, with EMET) over "Assassins" (LOGOS) or lowercase "assassins" (PNEUMA) -- the term is a proper historical designation; PNEUMA "recently" for PRO TOUTON TON HEMERON is too loose; PNEUMA drops article "the" before "four thousand" |
| 38 | "of-the Sicarii" | "of the Assassins" | "assassins" | "of the Sicarii" | SOPHIA preserves SIKARION as transliterated loanword -- it is a specific historical term for Jewish dagger-men, not a generic "assassins" |
| 38 | "having-stirred-up" | "stirred up a revolt" | "stirred up a revolt" | "stirred up a revolt" | LOGOS/PNEUMA supply "revolt" for ANASTATOSAS; EMET bare participle; "revolt" warranted by context |
| 39 | "I am a man indeed, a Jew, of-Tarsus of-Cilicia" | "I am a Jewish man, from Tarsus in Cilicia" | "I am a Jew, from Tarsus in Cilicia" | "I am a Jewish man, from Tarsus in Cilicia" | LOGOS combines ANTHROPOS IOUDAIOS as "Jewish man"; PNEUMA drops ANTHROPOS; EMET "a man indeed, a Jew" is awkward apposition |
| 39 | "of-no-insignificant city a citizen" | "a citizen of no insignificant city" | "a citizen of no insignificant city" | "a citizen of no insignificant city" | LOGOS/PNEUMA agree on word order; EMET inverted |
| 39 | "I-ask you, permit me to-speak to the people" | "I beg you, permit me to speak to the people" | "I ask you, let me speak to the people" | "I beg you, permit me to speak to the people" | LOGOS "beg" for DEOMAI is more precise (it connotes entreaty); PNEUMA "let me" less formal than "permit me"; EMET "ask" acceptable but weaker |
| 40 | "having-permitted him, Paul, having-stood upon the steps" | "when he had given him permission, Paul, standing on the steps" | "When the commander gave him permission, Paul stood on the steps" | "when he had given him permission, Paul, standing on the steps" | LOGOS temporal clause; PNEUMA makes subject explicit "the commander" (helpful but not in text); EMET double-participle |
| 40 | "motioned with-the hand to-the people" | "motioned with his hand to the people" | "motioned with his hand to the crowd" | "motioned with his hand to the people" | EMET/LOGOS "to the people" for TW LAW; PNEUMA "crowd" for LAOS (LAOS = people, not OCHLOS = crowd) |
| 40 | "much silence having-come-about" | "when a great silence had fallen" | "A deep silence fell" | "when a great silence had fallen" | LOGOS "great silence had fallen" natural; PNEUMA "deep silence fell" acceptable variant; EMET genitive absolute |
| 40 | "he-addressed [them] in the Hebrew language, saying" | "he addressed them in the Hebrew language, saying" | "he began to address them in the Hebrew language" | "he addressed them in the Hebrew language, saying" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "began to" and drops "saying" (LEGON) |

---

## Key Theological Disagreements

### 1. Verse 1 -- Patara (Textual Variant)
All three translators correctly followed the Sinaiticus reading PATARA alone, without the "and Myra" (KAI MURA) addition found in Codex D and some later witnesses. The shorter Alexandrian reading is adopted as the earlier and more reliable text. No disagreement among translators on this point.

### 2. Verse 4 -- Spirit's Warning vs. Command
The most theologically sensitive verse in the chapter. The Greek reads ELEGON TO PAULO DIA TOU PNEUMATOS ME EPIBAINEIN EIS IEROSOLUMA -- "they were saying to Paul through the Spirit not to set foot in Jerusalem." The tension is stark: the Spirit prompts the warning, yet Paul proceeds.
- **EMET:** "were-saying through the Spirit not to-set-foot" -- preserves tension literally
- **LOGOS:** "they kept telling Paul through the Spirit not to set foot" -- adds imperfective "kept telling"
- **PNEUMA:** "Through the Spirit they kept urging Paul not to set foot" -- "urging" is stronger than "telling/saying"
- **SOPHIA:** Follows LOGOS. The Spirit revealed the danger; the disciples interpreted this as prohibition; Paul distinguished prophecy of suffering from command to avoid. The consensus phrasing preserves the ambiguity without resolving it.

### 3. Verse 11 -- "The Jews" vs. "The Jewish leaders"
- **EMET/LOGOS:** "the Jews" (OI IOUDAIOI)
- **PNEUMA:** "the Jewish leaders" -- adds "leaders" not present in Greek
- **SOPHIA:** "the Jews" -- follows the text. PNEUMA's addition, while possibly reflecting a historical nuance, goes beyond the manuscript.

### 4. Verse 14 -- "The will of the Lord be done" (Gethsemane Echo)
All three translators preserved the allusion to Jesus's prayer in Gethsemane (Luke 22:42). Minor differences in word order:
- **EMET:** "The will of-the Lord let-it-be-done" (Greek word order: genitive-first)
- **LOGOS:** "The will of the Lord be done" (formal jussive)
- **PNEUMA:** "The Lord's will be done" (possessive restructuring)
- **SOPHIA:** Follows LOGOS. The formal jussive best renders GINESTHO in this solemn context.

### 5. Verse 20 -- "Myriads" / "Tens of Thousands" vs. "Thousands"
A significant lexical disagreement:
- **EMET:** "tens-of-thousands" (literal MURIADES)
- **LOGOS:** "thousands" (domesticated English)
- **PNEUMA:** "thousands" (domesticated English)
- **SOPHIA:** "tens of thousands" -- MURIADES specifically denotes multiples of 10,000. While it may be hyperbolic, the English "thousands" under-translates the Greek. The scale claim is theologically significant: it shapes the political pressure on Paul in the following verses.

### 6. Verse 25 -- The Apostolic Decree (Fourfold Form)
All three translators preserved the fourfold prohibition from Sinaiticus (idol-sacrifices, blood, what is strangled, sexual immorality), correctly representing the Alexandrian text against the Western text (Codex Bezae) which omits "what is strangled" and adds the negative Golden Rule. Differences were in phrasing:
- **EMET:** "idol-sacrifice and blood and strangled and sexual-immorality" (bare nouns)
- **LOGOS:** "what is sacrificed to idols and from blood and from what is strangled and from sexual immorality" (prepositional expansion)
- **PNEUMA:** "food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from the meat of strangled animals, and from sexual immorality" (interpretive additions: "food," "meat of...animals")
- **SOPHIA:** Follows LOGOS. The prepositional "from" before each item clarifies the Greek PHULASSESTHAI APO construction.

### 7. Verse 36 -- "Away with him!" (Passion Parallel)
All three translators rendered AIRE AUTON identically as "Away with him!" -- preserving the deliberate Lukan parallel to the cry against Jesus (Luke 23:18). No disagreement on this crucial intertextual echo.

### 8. Verse 38 -- "Sicarii" vs. "Assassins"
- **EMET:** "Sicarii" (transliterated)
- **LOGOS:** "Assassins" (translated, capitalized)
- **PNEUMA:** "assassins" (translated, lowercase)
- **SOPHIA:** "Sicarii" -- the Greek SIKARION is itself a Latin loanword referring to a specific Jewish revolutionary group, not generic assassins. Preserving the proper noun maintains the historical specificity of the Roman commander's accusation.

### 9. Verse 40 -- "Hebrew Language" (Hebrew or Aramaic?)
All three translators rendered TE HEBRAIDI DIALEKTO as "the Hebrew language/dialect." The referent is almost certainly Aramaic, the spoken Semitic language of first-century Palestine, but all three correctly preserved the text as written rather than substituting "Aramaic." SOPHIA concurs: the text says "Hebrew," and the translation follows the text.

### 10. Verse 30 -- "Doors" vs. "Gates"
- **EMET/LOGOS:** "doors" (THURAI)
- **PNEUMA:** "gates" -- and adds "slammed shut behind him"
- **SOPHIA:** "doors" -- THURAI means doors; PNEUMA's "gates" and added details are interpretive.

---

*End of Disagreement Log -- Acts Chapter 21*
